meta.title: W206 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes W206 engine — M264, M254, OM654, M139l. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the 48V mild-hybrid tech explained. Compare prices from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes C-Class W206 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned W206 engine typically costs £2,500–£18,000+ supply only, depending on the engine — the volume OM654 diesel and M264 petrol sit at £2,500–£5,000, while the M139l in AMG models ranges from £8,000–£18,000+. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W206 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654.820 diesel (C220d/C300d) and the M264.915 petrol (C180/C200) are the most dependable. The OM654 is a proven, robust diesel with a 200,000-mile-plus lifespan. The M264 is a carryover from the W205 era, making it the safer bet compared to the newer M254. The M254 has known oil consumption and camshaft adjuster issues that are still being resolved.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes C-Class W206?

acceptedAnswer.text: M254 oil consumption and camshaft adjuster failure (confirmed by Mercedes internal bulletin), 48V mild-hybrid system issues including ISG and battery failures, OM654 turbocharger failure on early units (2021–2022), and electrical system/software glitches affecting infotainment and driver assistance systems.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes C-Class W206 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on the engine. The OM654 diesel and M264 petrol are genuinely robust and well-documented. The M254 is newer and has reported teething issues — early cars (2021–2022) appear most at risk. The 48V mild-hybrid system is a new technology for the C-Class and has some reported issues. The M139l AMG unit is high-performance and expensive to maintain.

mainEntity.name: Which W206 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but the M254.920 (C300, C300e, C350e) is the one we'd advise caution on. Mercedes has issued an internal bulletin confirming camshaft adjuster failure and oil consumption issues. If you buy one, ensure it has a full service history and check for any outstanding recalls or software updates.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my W206 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Camshaft-related fault codes (P001100, P001104, P001200) or a rattle on startup (M254), turbocharger noise or loss of power (OM654), or 48V system warning lights (all models) are key warnings. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes C-Class W206 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: The W206 is a current-generation car with strong retained values — a £3,000–£5,000 OM654 recon against a £20,000–£25,000 C220d is 12–25% of the car's value, safely below our 70% Mercedes threshold. Even the M254 at £3,500–£6,000 against a £22,000 C300 is 16–27%. The only exception is the AMG C63 — but if you can afford a £70,000+ car, you can afford the maintenance. Engine replacement is generally viable across the range.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes C-Class W206 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654 diesel and M264 petrol can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing. The M254 is too new for definitive longevity data, but early signs suggest it should reach 150,000+ miles if the known issues are addressed. The M139l in AMG models is typically good for 80,000–120,000 miles if maintained to AMG standards.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the W206, S206 and V206?

acceptedAnswer.text: All three share the fifth-generation C-Class platform and most engines. The W206 is the 4-door saloon (2021–present, this page), the S206 is the Estate (2021–present), and the V206 is the long-wheelbase saloon sold in China. Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access — see each generation's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes C-Class W206 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the block. Tell us your variant (e.g., C220d, C200, AMG C43) and registration year, and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes C-Class W206 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W206 saloon — from the M264 1.5-litre mild-hybrid to the M254 2.0-litre petrols, the OM654 diesels, and the electrified M139l in the AMG C43 and C63. All W206 engines are four-cylinders with 48V mild-hybrid assistance — Mercedes has dropped six-cylinders entirely from this generation. Backed by real UK W206 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes C-Class W206 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W206 saloon specifically — all are four-cylinder turbocharged units with 48V mild-hybrid assistance, marking the first time Mercedes has offered the C-Class without six-cylinder options .

engines.power: 350 kW (476 bhp) engine only; 500 kW (680 bhp) combined

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM654 diesel family: 80% confidence — well-documented Mercedes diesel family with strong UK specialist support, though early OM654 units had some turbocharger issues. M264: 75% confidence — proven engine from the W205 era, carried over with mild-hybrid additions. M254: 70% confidence — newer engine with some reported issues (oil consumption, camshaft adjusters) that are still being understood . M139l: 70% confidence — high-performance AMG unit derived from the A45's M139, but with a new electric turbocharger; long-term reliability is unproven .

overview.intro: The W206 is the fifth-generation C-Class, launched in March 2021 as the successor to the W205 . It sits on the MRA2 platform with a 100 kg weight reduction and a longer wheelbase . For the first time, every W206 C-Class is equipped with a four-cylinder engine with an integrated starter generator and a 48-volt electrical system — Mercedes has dropped six-cylinder engines entirely from this generation . The engine range spans the M264 1.5-litre petrol, the M254 2.0-litre petrol, the OM654 2.0-litre diesel, and the M139l in AMG models. Plug-in hybrid options (C300e, C350e, C63 S E-Performance) are available with a 25.4 kWh battery.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes MRA2 • Predecessor: W205 • Successor: — (current generation) • Sibling body styles in this generation: S206 Estate, V206 long-wheelbase saloon.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W206 C-Class is the current-generation model; enquiry volumes are growing as early cars (2021–2022) exit manufacturer warranty and enter the used market.

items.quote: The 2.0-litre OM654 is a proven, robust diesel with a genuine 200,000-mile-plus lifespan. The 48V mild-hybrid system adds refinement, and the C300d's 261bhp makes it genuinely quick. Early OM654 units had some turbocharger issues, but these appear to have been resolved by 2022. It's the W206's most dependable engine.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want diesel economy and torque without compromising on refinement.

items.quote: The 1.5-litre M264 offers 201bhp with 48V mild-hybrid assistance — it's smooth, refined, and more than enough for UK roads. The M264 is a proven engine from the W205 era, carried over with mild-hybrid additions, making it the safer bet compared to the newer M254.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a modern, efficient petrol without the M254's reliability question marks.

items.quote: The M254 is the newest engine in the W206 lineup, replacing the M264. But it comes with reported issues: excessive oil consumption, camshaft adjuster failure (confirmed by Mercedes internal bulletin LI05.20-P-080257), and variator problems at relatively low mileage . These are still being understood, and long-term reliability is unproven.
items.whoItsFor: buyers willing to accept teething issues for the latest technology.

items.quote: The M139l in the C63 is the most complex engine ever fitted to a C-Class — 2.0 litres, 476bhp from the engine alone, plus a rear-axle electric motor for 680bhp combined. When something goes wrong, it goes wrong expensively. The electric turbocharger, the hybrid system, and the sheer labour intensity of working on this engine mean replacement costs will be eye-watering.
items.whoItsFor: buyers with deep pockets who want the most powerful four-cylinder production car ever.

items.quote: The C220d is the volume diesel for good reason — 197bhp, genuine 50+ mpg, and the same robust OM654 as the more powerful C300d. It's cheaper to buy, cheaper to run, and cheaper to replace than the petrol alternatives. The sweet spot in the W206 range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the best combination of economy, performance, and reliability.

items.quote: The C180 is often overlooked in favour of the C200, but it's the same 1.5-litre M264 engine with 168bhp instead of 201bhp. It's cheaper to buy, cheaper to insure, and fundamentally the same reliable engine. For most UK buyers, 168bhp is more than enough.
items.whoItsFor: budget-conscious buyers who want a premium badge without the premium running costs.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Petrol (Value) — proven M264, mild-hybrid refinement.
PHEV — complex, expensive when it fails.
AMG Performance — fast, but expensive to maintain.
Most Expensive — the most complex C-Class ever.

economicsRule.title: The W206 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,000–£5,000 OM654 recon against a £25,000 W206 sits at 12–20% of the car's value — comfortably safe. Even the M254 at £3,500–£6,000 against a £22,000 C300 is 16–27% — well within our 70% Mercedes threshold. The W206 is a new-generation car with strong retained values; engine replacement is financially viable across the range. The only exception is the AMG C63 — but if you can afford a £70,000+ car, you can afford the maintenance.

problems.title: M254 Oil Consumption & Camshaft Adjuster Failure
problems.rootCause: The M254 engine has known issues with excessive oil consumption and camshaft adjuster (variator) failure. Mercedes-Benz internal bulletin LI05.20-P-080257 confirms this as a design defect affecting the M254 engine in all model series . Fault codes P001100, P001104, and P001200 are linked to structural failure of the wire mesh in the oil control valve .
problems.recommendation: If your M254 develops a rattle on startup, check engine light with camshaft-related codes, or requires frequent oil top-ups, get it inspected immediately — £800–£1,500 now is far cheaper than the £3,500–£6,000+ replacement that follows a catastrophic failure. Early W206 M254 engines (2021–2022) appear to be most at risk.

cta.label: Compare M254 engine prices for your W206 C300 →

problems.title: OM654 Turbocharger Failure (Early Units)
problems.rootCause: Early OM654 diesel units have reported turbocharger failures caused by increased exhaust backpressure . The turbocharger's rotating assembly can suffer from axial wear, leading to failure. This appears to have been resolved on later production units.
problems.recommendation: On any early W206 diesel (2021–2022) over 60,000 miles, have the turbocharger inspected at every service — £600–£1,000 now is far cheaper than the £3,000–£5,000 replacement that follows a catastrophic failure.

cta.label: Compare OM654 engine prices for your W206 C220d/C300d →

problems.title: W206 48V Mild-Hybrid System Issues
problems.affectedModels: All W206 variants with EQ Boost (all petrol and diesel models).
problems.rootCause: The 48V electrical system and integrated starter generator (ISG) are complex components — battery degradation, ISG failure, and software glitches have been reported. The 48V battery is expensive to replace when it fails.
problems.recommendation: The 48V system is a new technology for the C-Class — ensure your car has the latest software updates and have the battery health checked at every service. A £300–£600 check is far cheaper than the £1,800–£3,000 replacement that follows a complete failure.

problems.title: W206 Electrical System & Software Glitches
problems.rootCause: The W206 has accumulated 58 NHTSA complaints, with the most frequently reported problem areas being Safety Systems, Electrical System, and Brakes . Software glitches affecting the infotainment, driver assistance systems, and general electrical functions are common.
problems.recommendation: Ensure your W206 has the latest software updates from Mercedes — many electrical issues are resolved with a dealer software update. If you're buying used, check the service history for software update records.

replacementCosts.h2: W206 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page's baseline figures — W206 saloon shares identical engine-bay access with its S206 estate sibling, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required. M139l costs reflect AMG-specific parts, the electric turbocharger, and specialist labour requirements.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W206 saloon only. S206 Estate and V206 long-wheelbase saloon share most of these engines but are covered on their own Generation Pages, as fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style.
